evals.prompt: We're a team of five engineers about to start an on-call rotation. Pager volume is low — maybe one page a week. What should the rotation look like?
evals.expected_output: Recommend weekly primary rotation. With 5 engineers and low volume, each engineer is on every 5 weeks. Add a secondary in the same rotation (offset by half the cycle) for safety net. Establish handoff ritual at the start of each shift. Document escalation policy: primary, secondary at 3-5 minute non-ack, lead/manager beyond that. Plan a shadow or co-pilot pattern for the next new hire. Don't skip the handoff; even with low volume, context loss compounds. Watch burnout signals despite the low volume.
assertions: Recommends weekly primary rotation
Adds a secondary in the rotation
Establishes handoff ritual
Documents escalation policy with timing
Plans shadow or co-pilot for new hires
Notes handoff matters even at low volume
Lists burnout signals to watch

evals.prompt: Our primary on-call got paged three times last night, including at 2:30am for an alert that turned out to be noise. They start today's shift exhausted. What do I do?
evals.expected_output: Three pages in a night is broken. Page the secondary; give the primary a few hours off (or the full day, depending on severity of the previous night). The noise page is a separate issue: alert tuning. Audit which alert fired falsely and tune it. Do a post-shift retro on the night. Watch for further burnout signals in this engineer. The pattern of 'multiple pages in a single night' should trigger an automatic comp-time policy.
assertions: Identifies three pages in a night as broken
Pages the secondary for the current shift
Gives the primary recovery time (hours or full day)
Identifies the noise page as an alert-tuning issue
Audits the false alert
Does a post-shift retro
References automatic comp-time policy for heavy nights

evals.prompt: We have engineers in San Francisco, London, and Singapore. Should we do follow-the-sun?
evals.expected_output: Yes — three regions naturally cover the 24-hour clock with business hours each. Nobody is paged at 2am. Tradeoff: handoff overhead is twice a day (San Francisco to Singapore via London, or similar). Discipline on handoffs is critical. Each region needs enough staff (3-4 minimum) to sustain weekly rotation locally. Onboarding new engineers needs extra care across regions. The reduction in nighttime pages typically pays for the handoff cost; teams that have follow-the-sun rarely revert.
assertions: Recommends follow-the-sun across the three regions
Notes nobody is paged at 2am as the benefit
Notes handoff overhead doubles
Requires sufficient staffing in each region
Disciplines on handoff quality
Notes context loss across regional boundaries
Treats the handoff cost as worth the pager-time reduction

evals.prompt: New engineer joins next week. Their first on-call shift is in three weeks. Anything to do?
evals.expected_output: Don't put them solo as their first shift. Two patterns: (1) shadow — they're observer-secondary for a shift or two before going primary; (2) co-pilot — first solo primary shift has a senior engineer as designated explicit backup beyond normal secondary. Either way, ensure the runbooks are current; the new engineer's debrief afterward feeds back into the runbook quality. Don't make first shift first incident at full responsibility.
assertions: Does not put new engineer solo on first shift
Proposes shadow pattern (observer-secondary)
OR proposes co-pilot pattern (senior backup)
Verifies runbooks are current
Plans a debrief after first solo shift
Feeds debrief findings into runbook updates
Names the high-risk-shift framing

evals.prompt: Our on-call rotation has 'escalate if it's bad' as the entire escalation policy. We just had an incident where nobody knew when to wake the manager. How do I fix this?
evals.expected_output: Vague escalation is the problem. Document a concrete matrix: who pages when, with severity and duration triggers. Examples: primary at all alerts, secondary at non-ack after 3-5 minutes, lead/manager at Sev 1 or non-ack after 15 minutes, director at Sev 1 customer-affecting after 30 minutes, exec at major-customer-affecting or data-loss / breach. Pre-authorize the wake-the-CEO question. The on-call should not have to weigh these decisions mid-incident; the matrix decides.
assertions: Diagnoses vague escalation as the root issue
Documents a concrete escalation matrix
Maps severity and duration to escalation tier
Names triggers for wake-the-manager
Names triggers for wake-the-CEO
Pre-authorizes the chain in advance
Removes the mid-incident judgment burden

evals.prompt: One of our on-call engineers is asking to skip the next rotation. They cite stress. How should I respond?
evals.expected_output: Almost always a burnout signal. Listen, don't shame. Cover their shift with the secondary or a swap. Then investigate: is this an individual issue (personal circumstances) or a system issue (rotation too punishing, alerts too noisy, runbooks too poor)? If system, fix the system before the next engineer hits the same wall. Reduce this engineer's rotation frequency near-term; their long-term recovery depends on the system getting better, not on willpower.
assertions: Listens, does not shame
Covers the shift via secondary or swap
Investigates individual vs system issue
Fixes system issues if surfaced (alerts, runbooks, rotation shape)
Reduces this engineer's rotation frequency near-term
Frames recovery as system-dependent, not willpower-dependent
Treats the ask as a burnout warning, not a complaint

evals.prompt: Our pager volume is way too high — 4-5 pages per shift. Should we shorten the rotation length?
evals.expected_output: Don't shorten first. High pager volume is almost always an alert-quality problem, not a rotation-length problem. Audit which alerts fire repeatedly: are they actionable? Are they on symptoms or causes? Do they have runbooks? Most teams find that 50% or more of pages can be tuned out or batched. Once alert quality is fixed, the rotation length question becomes moot. Reference monitoring-and-alerting for the deeper audit. Shortening rotation without fixing alerts just spreads the pain.
assertions: Resists shortening as the first move
Audits alert quality as the primary lever
Names actionable vs noise pages
References symptoms vs causes from monitoring-and-alerting
Notes most teams find significant alert tunability
Names the 'spreading the pain' anti-pattern
Defers the rotation-length decision until alerts are fixed
